does: This paper studies consistency training as a defense against adversarial attacks on reasoning-capable LLMs, comparing output-level consistency training (BCT) with activation-level consistency training (ACT). The authors evaluate both methods across five reasoning models in prompt injection and jailbreak settings, using self-supervised pairs of clean and adversarially rewritten prompts. They demonstrate that ACT achieves competitive robustness against adaptive attacks while requiring minimal supervision. The paper provides mechanistic insights by showing that ACT encodes refusal behavior as a roughly linear shift in activation space at the assistant-turn boundary, enabling extraction of a single steering direction that controls model refusal with minimal impact on benign performance.
connects: This work extends the consistency training paradigm, previously applied to robustness in vision and standard language models, to the more complex setting of reasoning models with extended chain-of-thought outputs. It builds on prior adversarial defense research including prompt injection mitigation and jailbreak robustness, while incorporating recent understanding of mechanistic interpretability in LLMs. The activation-level approach parallels work on representation-based defenses and steering, connecting to research on understanding and controlling model behavior through activation space manipulations. The paper responds to the challenge that reasoning models' longer outputs create new attack surfaces, extending defenses beyond simple prompt-response architectures.
enables: This work opens directions for more interpretable and mechanistically grounded adversarial defenses in reasoning models. The discovery that ACT defenses manifest as learnable linear directions in activation space enables future work on steering, control, and understanding refusal mechanisms without extensive fine-tuning. The findings suggest potential applications for efficient defense deployment and could facilitate development of more robust reasoning models. The mechanistic insights may enable better detection and understanding of adaptive attacks, while the steering approach could be extended to other safety-critical model behaviors, pointing toward interpretable alignment techniques for increasingly capable reasoning systems.
